response:
In my last job, I ended up running the furlough scheme during lockdown. I worked for a theatre, and we were closed; almost everyone was on furlogh except me and the finance director. I was exhausted; no days off for months long hours, and managing communications with very stressed people.

I had a month long break to use up my time off in lieu, but it wasn't enough. I took a career break; stopped work altogether, ate well, exercised, visited people I care about.

When I felt ready a few months later, I signed up for a college course in counselling, which I have now finished; and I started volunteering at Citizen's Advice, which I still do now. I learned that for me, it's not about the money it's about being able to help people. And I need to be healthy in body and mind to do that.

Any job I look at now, has to feed my soul, not just my bank account.

response: Last Sunday I went to see the new Superman movie. Truly, it was one of the best movies I've seen recently (and I've seen a lot of bad movies recently- especially in theaters). The movie started off intense, and stayed intense throughout. What I can appreciate the most, is that it had a sense of humor within the movie that didn't take itself too seriously. It didn't feel too heavy. I really enjoyed being able to laugh, all while being stressed about the main plot (but we all know superheros always win... wink). If you're a fan of the Deadpool movies from Marvel, the comedy plus serious action/plot, you'll be a fan of this new Superman movie. David Corenswet was the best Superman in my opinion thus far, not taking himself too seriously but still being that hero we all know and love. The side characters were hilarious, the sub plots were just as entertaining as the main plot, and I really felt a soft spot for Superman's character as I too grew up in the country midwest.

response: I've always loved horror movies, even since I was a little kid hiding behind the sofa with my eyes glued to the screen. There's something about them that makes your heart race, it gets your blood flowing and emotions are running high. A lot of people feel the same, which is why horror is one of the most popular genres. It has a loyal fan base, and has relatively low production costs in terms of actually filming/making the movie. That's why I'm presenting my case for the $10,000 to help me create the horror movie of my dreams. The story is simple, a girl is taken but wakes up in what is seemingly her home. Turns out that her stalker has been watching her every move since even before she was born, creating an exact replica of the inside of her home- in his home. The story is her trying to fight for her freedom, a mystery on who this person is that took her, how they are involved with her family, and uncovering this giant web of uncertainty. With $10,000, I would be able to cover production costs, and be able to find a cast that I think fits perfectly for this film. Concepts like these are popular on Netflix, Hulu, etc. I plan to distribute my film at festivals, hopefully to see it on the big screen one day.

response: Last April, my life changed for the worst. My Grandma was like my mother. She picked me up from school, went to all of my concerts, taught me how to drive, and loved me fiercely. In 2021 I found out she had kidney cancer that had gone undetected for years. At the time we found out, it was too late and she was already terminal. We knew we had limited time with her, but we weren't sure exactly how long she had left. It was a horrible 3 years. Chemo, immunotherapy, brain surgery for a tumor that metastasized on her brain, drainage tubes coming out of her lungs, you name it. In April of 2024 she took her last breaths in her living room, on hospice. Getting the call from my Dad telling me she passed away felt like a wave of sadness, but some relief because I knew she wasn't suffering anymore. Her death was and still is the biggest challenge I've faced. Getting closer to God, knowing my Grandma is with me even though I can't see her, and remembering her has helped so much. Her death has taught me to be resilient in the face of uncertainty. To be proud of myself no matter what. To go to the doctor if something feels wrong. To love unconditionally even if I'm scared.

response:
I got into a car crash...

One moment I was driving in a straight line, the next moment, I was in a ditch. The sounds of tires screeching, airbags deploying, and shattering glass overwhelmed my senses. I still remember the smell of tires and airbags to this day. Thankfully, everyone was totally fine, no injuries, but it made me think about how fragile life can be. It's not every day that you're in a car crash, and you never think it can happen to you, until it does.

Everyone is trying their best, but at times you can forget that the other driver is also a human being with their own life. Accidents happen to everyone, and no matter what happens, it's important to be understanding of everyone involved. How do YOU stay mindful and present on the road?

response:
Our lives are becoming more and more remote, remote work, remote friends, remote love life. While there are many ways to interact in a business or gaming context, the ability to share the smaller moments in life is being lost. For that reason, I want to try and fill some of that loss with VR, by integrating augmented reality so that partners can see themselves sharing dinner together even while they're leagues apart. Both partners would setup their VR environment with a table and a meal in front of them, with a 3d-capuring camera pointed at them from the other side of the table, and they'd project their image to one another. This way they can enjoy their meal with company, prompting the idle little casual chats which are the heart of any intimate relationship and for which we sometimes forget to make time for. 

The software could also sell DLC for more exotic backdrops for the couple to enjoy while sharing their meal, turning it from a night in to a fancy night out.

response: I know, you hear the phrase "bakery" and think, "There's another $10,000 down the drain". Bakeries, like restaurants, seem to come and go so quickly; everything from the home baker selling their goods on Facebook to the artisan bakery selling $12 loaves of bread. So what makes mine unique? Simplicity, quality, and low costs. An artisan bakery doesn't need to sell $12 loaves of bread; a well made loaf can be made for a quarter of that, and sold for half while still turning a profit. For too long people have needed to buy cheap, poor quality mass produced baked goods at supermarkets, or go to premium, high end local bakeries, but I aim to meet them in the middle, without compromising on quality. This will redefine what the neighbourhood bakery is by offering affordability and quality in every item.

response: It wouldn't be fair for me to say that COVID was difficult on me; I was one of the lucky ones, who was on a wage subsidy and who didn't get sick (nor did any of my immediate family). However, it did provide a significant challenge, as I had started at my previous job just one week before it hit. At first it had felt like a dream; I had finally been hired and given a start date for my dream job, one that I'd specifically focused on throughout university. When that time finally came though, and my training began, the world felt completely different. Gone were the in-person training sessions, or chances to meet co-workers and get to know them on a more personal level, and that was replaced with virtual meetings and email greetings. Starting work on projects was an immediate challenge without that in-person introduction as I felt like I wasn't part of the group, and other people didn't know me enough to tell me when things should be done differently. I knew that needed to change before I could fully contribute to the company, and so I focused on getting to know each person on my team, through one on one video calls for each and every one. That, combined with a focus on communication where I'd be consistently proactively helping, led me to become an accepted member of the team despite the difficult and unique circumstances.

response: Last week, my dad told me that I am not just his daughter, but his friend. To those with loving, doting fathers who never shy away from verbalizing their verbalizing their feelings, this statement might seem insignificant. But to me, it meant the whole world. My father is a typical African man. Raised in a culture where men have no choice but to be macho and unemotional under the guise of being manly, he never truly verbalized that he liked my company. I mean, I knew he loved me and was proud of me. But, friends? It's so common in African, particularly west-African families that children are never to be viewed as friends but subordinates. My mom has exemplified this concept. That being said, hearing him say, in a way in which I have never heard before, that I am not just his daughter who he has no choice but to raise, love, and care for but his friend that he admires and chooses to spend time with means more than words can express.

response: I've been a recovering alcoholic since I was a teenager.
